title: Testing, Static Quality, Performance, and Release · JavaScript Performance Board
summary: Solve unfamiliar JavaScript runtime, data, asynchronous, module, DOM, accessibility, security, testing, and release incidents without workshop solutions. Planned artifact: Produce a cumulative evidence packet across unfamiliar cases, performance tasks, explanations, and root-cause repairs. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish ECMAScript language behavior from browser, worker, and server host APIs.
Record runtime, module mode, inputs, locale, time, and dependency conditions needed to reproduce behavior.
Handle IEEE-754 precision, NaN, Infinity, safe integers, parsing, rounding, and decimal-sensitive decisions.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.javascript:
// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.
// Add one runnable behavior, assertion, changed case, and evidence return per activity.

steps.title: Plan the cumulative evidence run
steps.instruction: Order the Runtime Host evidence cycle for this exam task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Runtime Host assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Runtime Host has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Runtime Host precondition or invariant that the exam case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: JavaScript and the browser are the same runtime specification.

options.text: Declare inputs, observable output, ownership, and the runtime behavior being tested
Trace values, bindings, receivers, mutations, and queued work before editing code
Implement the smallest behavior and retain an assertion over the actual result
Run boundary and changed-event cases, inspect failures, and remove accidental coupling

stimulus.title: Async Testing case record
stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: assumption
lines.text: A release candidate passes the familiar 2-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: Adding a delay is a reliable way to test asynchronous completion.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Await asynchronous outcomes, control timers and network fixtures, and fail on unhandled work deterministically. Artifact under review: a tested and release-ready JavaScript application.

options.text: Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
The record names the assumption, applies Async Testing, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.
The record says the result looks right but supplies no quantity, trace, condition, or counterexample.

steps.title: Choose the regression defense
steps.instruction:
exam case — A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

A polished result passes one familiar case but fails a boundary case. What is the defensible release decision?

content.text: A release candidate passes the familiar 4-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Async Testing symptom.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: MDN Web API Reference — Controls DOM, events, workers, fetch, storage, abort, and browser security boundary coverage.

stimulus.title: Async Testing failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Adding a delay is a reliable way to test asynchronous completion.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Await asynchronous outcomes, control timers and network fixtures, and fail on unhandled work deterministically.

options.text: Ignore the boundary result because the primary demonstration passed.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
Stop release, localize the cause, repair the smallest responsible layer, and rerun the full changed-case set.

steps.title: Submit the certification defense
steps.instruction: Write the exam evidence note for Async Testing: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A release candidate passes the familiar 5-record fixture but fails an empty list and a duplicate identifier. Reproduce both, add behavior and accessibility regressions, and reject release until the clean build agrees.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Await asynchronous outcomes, control timers and network fixtures, and fail on unhandled work deterministically.
